results.possibleIdentity: Michael Kirby (1931–1997) was a prominent artist, theorist, and writer who was deeply embedded in the New York avant-garde scene during the 1960s and 1970s. He is best known for his foundational role in documenting and theorizing 'Happenings' and early performance art. Kirby authored the seminal book 'Happenings' in 1965 and 'The Art of Time: Essays on the Avant-Garde' in 1969. His work was characterized by a commitment to 'objective' documentation, often utilizing a scientific or rationalist approach to analyze art, which aligned with the interdisciplinary goals of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). He was a vociferous chronicler of E.A.T.-associated artists such as Robert Whitman, Claes Oldenburg, and Jim Dine. Beyond his theoretical work, he was a sculptor, filmmaker, and performer, appearing with the Wooster Group and founding his own 'Structuralist Workshop.' From 1971 to 1985, he served as the editor of 'The Drama Review' (TDR) and was a professor of performance studies at New York University's Tisch School of the Arts.

results.possibleIdentity: Roberts Scott Blossom (March 25, 1924 – July 8, 2011), often referred to as Bob Blossom in artistic and Beat circles, was an American character actor, poet, and multimedia artist. He was highly active in the New York avant-garde scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s. Blossom is notably associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), having participated as an artist and performer in the seminal 1966 event '9 Evenings: Theatre & Engineering.' During the 1960s, he founded and led 'Filmstage,' a pioneering multimedia theatrical troupe that integrated live performance with film projections and slides. His work in 'expanded cinema' placed him alongside other experimental figures like Robert Rauschenberg, Robert Whitman, and Stan VanDerBeek. In addition to his performance art, he was a prolific poet, appearing in 'The Outlaw Bible of American Poetry' and traveling with Beat figures such as Jack Micheline and Ray Bremser. While widely recognized for his film roles in 'Home Alone' (as Old Man Marley), 'Close Encounters of the Third Kind,' and 'Escape from Alcatraz,' he was a respected stage actor who won four Obie Awards for his Off-Broadway work.
